title: Guided Model Flight
description: Vector-stabilize your printed rocket and capture flight footage.

dialogue.text: Nova here! Instead of chasing suborbital leaps, let's add servo guidance to our 3D-printed rocket. Ready to build something just one step beyond our parachute launch?

options.text: Absolutely—what parts do we print first?

dialogue.text: We'll 3D print a fincan with servo pockets and a camera sled for the payload bay. Get both runs queued so the hardware matches our checklists.

options.text: Both prints cooled and cleaned up.

dialogue.text: Mount the micro servo and linkages so the fins can nudge the thrust line. Keep the wiring tidy—we'll route it up to the guidance bay next.

options.text: Install the servo and linkage kit
Servo swings smoothly across the travel range.

dialogue.text: Time to assemble the guidance controller: Arduino, IMU, and jumper harness, plus a quick sweep test so we know the firmware drives the servo correctly.

options.text: Solder and flash the guidance controller
Run the servo sweep test sketch

dialogue.text: Let's stack everything: fincan, body tube, parachute, camera sled, motor, and controller harness. Route the servo lead so it clears the ejection charge.

options.text: Airframe buttoned up with the payload secured.

dialogue.text: On site we insert the igniter, continuity-check the controller, and work through the laminated launch checklist. Range safety wants a sign-off before we call the countdown.

options.text: Complete the guided launch pad checks
Checklist signed, winds within limits, controller armed.

dialogue.text: Fire the motor and let the controller keep the rocket vertical while the camera records. Recovery crews will grab the capsule as soon as the parachute settles.

options.text: Telemetry packet and flight credit secured.

dialogue.text: Beautiful work! That vectoring kept the boost phase tidy, and the camera footage will help us tune the next airframe.

options.text: Export the footage and log the mission.
